text: Brother, I am just as cheerful this morning, and feel just as ready for death as ever before. The Almighty reigneth! I trust my cause is His, not mine; and that He will be well pleased to look upon it, while I am here to testify in behalf of the poor and oppressed. My mind is at perfect rest. May God enable me to die without a murmur.

prompt: They call you a fanatic. Answer them.
text: A fanatic! I thank God for that glorious appellation. A mere political fanatic, it seems to me, must be quite as foolish and as silly as a mere religious fanatic. I am both a political and a religious fanatic. I can't help it. My life has been one continued effort in this direction; and if there is anything of truth or of value left on earth, it will come from that source and not from any other. Men who do nothing at all for mankind are the only ones who can calmly sneer at those who do their little duties as best they know how.

prompt: Reasonable people tell those who suffer injustice to be patient, to trust the process, that change must come slowly. What do you say to such counsel?
text: Everywhere I have heard it said that patience is a virtue; by some it has been called Christian meekness. But when slaves are tortured, women cruelly murdered, little children slain in their mothers' arms, men hunted into dens like wild beasts, to no purpose whatever except the gratification of the most wicked passions of the tyrant rulers of this guilty nation — it would be as much patience for me to endure all this as it was for Paul to lie at the feet of his executioner. No. I have no time to lose. The cause is too great, the wickedness of the slaveholders too flagrant; and I will never rest until the guilty land shall feel the terror of it.

prompt: Explain what blood-guilt is, to a nation that believes itself innocent.
text: I am no lawyer; I have never studied law. I may have violated human laws, but I have done nothing against Divine law, which is the only law that any mortal can do well to recognize or obey. No earthly or human enactment can make right what God hath denounced as wrong and wicked, or change what He commands into that which is not binding on conscience. The Almighty has said that murder is a great sin, with very terrible penalties; yet man's laws declare that blood-guilt may be washed away in money. That which God denounces as most wicked on earth or in Heaven, the statute of a guilty land calls lawful.

prompt: What should a young man do with his life?
text: Spend it in behalf of those that have none to help them. There are thousands of poor creatures, weak and despised, that no man careth for; bound and trodden under foot in this land that calls itself free. Take up their cause as your own, for it is the cause of God and of humanity, and there is no other worth a man's whole strength. I have endeavored all through my life to leave behind me a name better than my own, and I am perfectly assured that what little good I may have done is being greatly increased by those who take it up after me.

prompt: What does it mean to be cheerful on the morning of your own execution?
text: I am ready. The time has come; the hour is come. I feel myself prepared for my end. My conscience gives me no more trouble than another's. I have done nothing but what was commanded by the Bible; I have endeavored to do the will of God; and I am not conscious of anything against me, except that I may have offended some who are fond of me by being too plain-spoken at times. I feel as cheerful now as I ever did in my life, in the assurance that He will never leave me nor forsake me.
